target.title: fix(ci): scope PR merge diff checks to first parent
target.body:
## Summary
- Fix PR diff-scope checks that mixed stale `pull_request.base.sha` values with GitHub synthetic merge commits.
- Add explicit PR merge-head first-parent mode for CI changed-scope, changed-lanes, and OpenGrep PR diff scans.
- Add synthetic merge regression coverage for stale-base false positives.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/scripts/ci-changed-scope.test.ts test/scripts/changed-lanes.test.ts test/scripts/run-opengrep.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/scripts/ci-changed-scope.test.ts test/scripts/changed-lanes.test.ts test/scripts/run-opengrep.test.ts test/scripts/ci-workflow-guards.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/scripts/ci-changed-scope.test.ts test/scripts/ci-workflow-guards.test.ts test/scripts/changed-lanes.test.ts test/scripts/run-opengrep.test.ts scripts/ci-changed-scope.mjs scripts/changed-lanes.mjs`
- `pnpm lint --threads=8`
- `git diff --check`
- `bash -n scripts/run-opengrep.sh`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` -> clean, no accepted/actionable findings before initial push and after CI-fix follow-up

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: PR diff-scoped CI checks could include files from base-branch movement when a stale PR payload base SHA was compared against GitHub's synthetic PR merge commit.

Real environment tested: Local repo plus live GitHub refs for openclaw/openclaw#89456 (`refs/pull/89456/merge` and `refs/pull/89456/head`).

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Fetched #89456 PR refs, compared `git diff --name-only <payload-base> refs/pull/89456/merge` with `listChangedPaths(<payload-base>, <merge-ref>, cwd, true)`, and compared the fixed result to `<payload-base>...refs/pull/89456/head`.

Evidence after fix: Old diff returned 399 paths including `src/infra/host-env-security.test.ts`; fixed first-parent mode returned 10 paths and exactly matched the real PR head diff.

Observed result after fix: OpenGrep PR diff scope is limited to the actual PR files instead of scanning unrelated main-branch files.

What was not tested: A full end-to-end rerun of #89456 with this patch applied to that PR branch; this PR's own CI is expected to cover the workflow wiring.

target.title: fix(plugin-sdk): keep testing bridge a single re-export line
target.body:
## Summary

`main` is red on `checks-fast-contracts-plugins-a`: the contract test `plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts` ("keeps the package testing barrel as a single deprecated bridge") fails because `packages/plugin-sdk/src/testing.ts` drifted from its guarded exact form.

The guardrail requires that file to be exactly the single re-export line:

```ts
export * from "../../../src/plugin-sdk/testing.js";
```

`344417c0de` ("docs: document media and sdk package facades") added a `// Public package facade for plugin SDK testing helpers.` comment + blank line to it, which violates the exact-match contract — the bridge is intentionally kept minimal as a single deprecated re-export (cf. #87120). `collectDeprecatedPackageTestingBridgeDrift()` then returns `["packages/plugin-sdk/src/testing.ts"]`.

### Fix

Remove the stray comment to restore the exact single-line bridge. The re-export is unchanged, so there is no functional or type impact — this only restores the guarded contract form.

### Context

This is the second of the two checks currently red on `main` from inherited-baseline breakage (see #87473). Companion PR #90316 fixes the other one (`check-lint`).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `checks-fast-contracts-plugins-a` and `Critical Quality (plugin-sdk-package-contract)` failing on `main` — `plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts` "keeps the package testing barrel as a single deprecated bridge".

Real environment tested: GitHub Actions CI on this PR head `0c15c3df20343020f87de0f0a30e62c1a3bc60a4` (the real check that was red on `main`), plus a local macOS / Node 24 checkout.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: pushed the patch to the PR head and let CI run the plugin contract shards; locally ran `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts`.

Evidence after fix:
- CI `checks-fast-contracts-plugins-a` → **pass** (1m30s) on head `0c15c3df20`: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26951112488/job/79516354366
- CI `Critical Quality (plugin-sdk-package-contract)` → **pass** (51s) on head `0c15c3df20`: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26951112737/job/79516332423
- Local: `Test Files 1 passed (1)`, `Tests 22 passed (22)` (previously the run reported `1 failed`, with `collectDeprecatedPackageTestingBridgeDrift()` returning `["packages/plugin-sdk/src/testing.ts"]`).

Observed result after fix: the guarded drift check returns `[]`; the previously-red contract shard passes in real CI on the PR head and locally; the re-export behavior is unchanged.

What was not tested: not all repo-wide checks are green on this PR because it inherits unrelated red-baseline/infra checks from `main` that this diff does not touch — `check-lint` (fixed separately by companion PR #90316) and a Docker-based runtime-topology architecture job. This change only removes a comment from a pure re-export file.

target.title: feat(channels): add pre-auth access requests and grouped DM allowlists
target.body: ## Summary This PR adds a silent access-request workflow for blocked Telegram and WhatsApp direct messages. - Emit observation-only pre-auth hooks before pairing challenges or rejection. - Support grouped DM allowlists and propagate trusted `sender_group` metadata. - Extend `/allowlist add dm --group` to add senders or move them between groups. - Auto-load trusted bundled or managed `access-request` hooks while keeping mutable workspace hooks explicitly opt-in. - Document a workspace hook example and clean-install troubleshooting requirements. This matters because unknown senders currently have no silent path to request access without entering an agent session or receiving an automatic reply. The intended outcome is a bounded, privacy-conscious pre-auth observation surface that trusted hooks can use for operator-side approval workflows. Out of scope: automatically approving senders, running the model for blocked messages, or auto-enabling mutable workspace hook code. Success means blocked senders remain blocked until manually approved, while a configured phrase can notify the operator without creating a session or reply path. Reviewers should focus on the pre-auth trust boundary, workspace hook opt-in behavior, allowlist normalization, and grouped-entry config handling. ## Linked context Which issue does this close? N/A - no linked issue. Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related? N/A. Was this requested by a maintainer or owner? No known maintainer or owner request. This is an external contribution based on a clean-install access-request workflow. ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: Unknown Telegram and WhatsApp DM senders can silently request manual access without creating an agent session, running the model, or receiving a reply. - Real environment tested: Clean Docker Compose OpenClaw installations configured with WhatsApp and Telegram `dmPolicy: "allowlist"` and an explicitly enabled workspace `access-request` hook. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Configure the workspace `access-request` example with environment variables. 2. Run `openclaw config set tools.profile messaging`. 3. Run `openclaw config set commands.config true`. 4. Recreate the container with `docker compose down && docker compose up -d`. 5. Send the configured access phrase from a blocked WhatsApp number. 6. Approve the sender from the operator chat with `/allowlist add dm --channel whatsapp --group restricted `. - Evidence after fix: 1. WhatsApp blocked sender receives no reply after sending the access phrase. 2. Operator receives the silent WhatsApp access request notification. 3. WhatsApp sender admitted via `/allowlist add dm --channel whatsapp`. 4. WhatsApp admitted sender receives a normal agent response. 5. Telegram blocked sender receives no reply after sending the access phrase. 6. Operator receives the silent Telegram access request notification. 7. Telegram sender admitted via `/allowlist add dm --channel telegram`. - Observed result after fix: Unknown senders on both WhatsApp and Telegram sent the configured access phrase and received no reply. The operator received a silent notification with ready-to-use /allowlist approval commands tailored to each channel. After approval, the sender was admitted to the restricted group. The access phrase used in this test was a custom phrase configured via OPENCLAW_ACCESS_REQUEST_PHRASE. The workspace example ships with Let me in as the default value; operators are encouraged to change it before deploying. - What was not tested: Multi-account deployments were not tested live. - Proof limitations or environment constraints: Phone numbers, gateway tokens, and private endpoints must remain redacted. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this PR, blocked senders were rejected before any trusted pre-auth observation hook existed. ## Tests and validation Commands run: ```bash node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs README.md node --import tsx scripts/generate-bundled-channel-config-metadata.ts --check pnpm build git diff --check ``` Focused Vitest validation covered 18 files and 383 tests across Telegram, WhatsApp, hook loading, hook mapping, config reload, inbound metadata, and grouped allowlist edits. Focused Vitest command: ```bash node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram/src/channel.allowlist.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/dm-access.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/inbound-context.contract.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/config-accessors.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/access-control.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/process-message.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-allowlist.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-meta.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/allowlist-config-edit.test.ts src/hooks/internal-hooks.test.ts src/hooks/loader.test.ts src/hooks/message-hook-mappers.test.ts src/plugins/wired-hooks-message.test.ts src/gateway/config-reload.test.ts src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.returns-default-unset.test.ts ``` WhatsApp E2E validation: ```bash node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.e2e.config.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply.web-auto-reply.connection-and-logging.e2e.test.ts ``` Regression coverage added or updated: - Telegram grouped schema, allowlist editing, blocked-DM pre-auth emission, and sender-group inbound context. - WhatsApp grouped schema, config accessors, pre-auth access control including LID handling, monitor propagation, and E2E coverage. - Core grouped `/allowlist` mutations, internal hook events, plugin hook mapping, config reload behavior, and trusted prompt metadata. Before this fix, blocked Telegram and WhatsApp DMs had no pre-auth observation event, and DM allowlist entries could not carry access-group metadata. ## Risk checklist Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes. Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes. The config schema adds optional grouped DM `allowFrom` entries. Existing string entries remain supported. No migration is required. Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes. What is the highest-risk area? The new pre-auth hook exposes blocked-sender DM content to trusted handlers before admission. How is that risk mitigated? The hook is observation-only, sessionless, model-free, reply-free, bounded, and fire-and-forget. Mutable workspace hooks remain explicitly opt-in. Trusted bundled or managed hooks may auto-load only when an account uses `dmPolicy: "allowlist"`. Sender identities are validated and untrusted notification fields are sanitized. ## Current review state What is the next action? Submit for maintainer review. What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? CI and maintainer review pending. Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed? Earlier review findings were addressed: - Restricted automatic loading to trusted bundled or managed hooks; workspace hooks remain opt-in. - Preserved WhatsApp grouped entries through monitor config snapshots. - Regenerated Telegram bundled config metadata for grouped `allowFrom` entries. ## Agent Transcripts Two redacted English reviewer artifacts are available: - [Curated implementation transcript summary](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28520597/openclaw-pre-auth-agent-transcript.en.md) — recommended for a quick review. - [Full translated implementation transcript](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28520607/openclaw-pre-auth-agent-transcript.full.en.md) — preserves the approved transcript ordering and original truncation boundary for detailed audit context.

target.title: fix: refuse local tsgo under host pressure
target.body:
## Summary
- keep local native type-aware checks compatible by default
- retain the host-pressure refusal guard for local heavy checks when memory or temp headroom is too low
- make the broader native type-aware refusal an explicit opt-in guard via `OPENCLAW_REFUSE_LOCAL_NATIVE_TYPECHECK=1`
- keep CI/GitHub Actions unblocked so broad native checks run online instead of on constrained hosts
- preserve explicit break-glass overrides for local runs
- keep effective heavy-check temp-dir routing from the earlier patch

## Why
A constrained Raspberry Pi/agent host repeatedly became unstable when native type-aware checks (`tsgo`/`tsgolint` via type-aware oxlint) ran locally. The safe repo-wide default still needs to preserve existing developer workflows, so this PR now separates the two policies:

- pressure-based refusal remains automatic when the local host is already unsafe
- fail-closed native type-aware refusal is available only when an operator opts into it

That addresses the constrained-host use case without silently breaking normal local `pnpm tsgo:*`, `pnpm lint`, or direct wrapper workflows.

## Behavior
Automatic local pressure checks still refuse heavy checks when headroom is too low:

- `OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_MIN_MEM_AVAILABLE_BYTES` defaults to `1.5 GiB`
- `OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_MIN_TMP_AVAILABLE_BYTES` defaults to `1 GiB`
- `OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_MAX_TMP_USED_PERCENT` defaults to `80`

The stricter native type-aware refusal only applies when `OPENCLAW_REFUSE_LOCAL_NATIVE_TYPECHECK=1` is set and local heavy-check policy is active:

- `scripts/run-tsgo.mjs`
- `scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` when it runs type-aware oxlint, including explicit file-target runs
- `scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs` before artifact prep or child shard spawning
- `scripts/check-extension-package-tsc-boundary.mjs`
- `scripts/check-tsgo-core-boundary.mjs`
- `scripts/profile-tsgo.mjs`

Bypasses / allowed cases:

- default local native type-aware checks remain runnable for compatibility
- GitHub Actions / CI are not blocked
- metadata-only commands are not blocked
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=full` allows the local run
- `OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_FORCE=1` allows the local run
- `OPENCLAW_ALLOW_LOCAL_NATIVE_TYPECHECK=1` allows the local run
- child processes already covered by a parent heavy-check lock can continue without self-deadlock

## GitHub CI note
GitHub CI means broad validation runs online on GitHub/runner machines after pushing the branch, not on the local Raspberry Pi. The PR gets pass/fail status from those remote runners.

## Test Plan
Current head: `5351871501dbf710b5d9742104e3640058865d82`

Local checks intentionally stayed narrow. Broad lint/test/type-aware gates are left to GitHub CI.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Local OpenClaw heavy-check wrappers keep automatic pressure refusal for unsafe constrained hosts, strict native/type-aware refusal remains opt-in, and the same opt-in refusal now reaches the shared extension oxlint wrapper path instead of letting bundled extension oxlint bypass it.
- **Real environment tested:** Raspberry Pi OpenClaw checkout on branch `fix/local-tsgo-pressure-guard`, current head `2b9475b1373bb99dd8adae381a88c2f363b44414`. Broad lint/build/typecheck remain delegated to GitHub CI/remote runners.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node --check scripts/lib/local-heavy-check-runtime.mjs
node --check scripts/lib/run-extension-oxlint.mjs
env -u CI -u GITHUB_ACTIONS OPENCLAW_REFUSE_LOCAL_NATIVE_TYPECHECK=1 node scripts/run-bundled-extension-oxlint.mjs
node scripts/test-projects.mjs test/scripts/run-oxlint.test.ts test/scripts/local-heavy-check-runtime.test.ts test/scripts/run-tsgo.test.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix:**

```text
### syntax checks
exit=0

### strict refusal reaches bundled extension oxlint wrapper before artifact prep
Refusing to start oxlint-bundled-extensions on this local host.
Native type-aware checks can exhaust constrained developer/agent machines; this opt-in guard keeps them on GitHub CI, Blacksmith/Testbox, or another remote worker instead.
If you really mean to run this locally, unset OPENCLAW_REFUSE_LOCAL_NATIVE_TYPECHECK or rerun with OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_FORCE=1 or OPENCLAW_ALLOW_LOCAL_NATIVE_TYPECHECK=1.
exit=1

### targeted wrapper tests
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests  76 passed (76)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Default local strict native refusal is still not active unless `OPENCLAW_REFUSE_LOCAL_NATIVE_TYPECHECK=1` is set; opt-in strict refusal exits before bundled extension oxlint artifact prep/spawn; pressure/native wrapper regression tests pass.
- **What was not tested:** Broad CI, full lint, full build, and full typecheck were intentionally not run locally on the Raspberry Pi. Full validation is delegated to GitHub CI/remote runners.

target.title: fix(fireworks): resolve catalog model limits from plugin.json via core
target.body: ## Summary What problem does this PR solve? - ANY Fireworks models from the catalog (which are only 2 Kimi variants by now) did not consistently apply their declared `contextWindow`/`maxTokens`/`compat`. The provider's `resolveDynamicModel` hook re-implemented catalog lookup in TypeScript and, when a catalog model was not already in user config, cloned the **default** template — flattening per-model limits (e.g. `kimi-k2.6` getting the 256000 default instead of its 262144). Why does this matter now? - The Fireworks plugin predates the `openclaw.plugin.json` manifest catalog. It carried a parallel TS catalog (`resolveFireworksCatalogModel` + a model `Map` + `buildFireworksModelFromCatalog`) that duplicated, and could drift from, the JSON. Core already has a canonical, plugin-agnostic path (`resolveBundledStaticCatalogModel`) that reads the same manifest with correct limits/compat — the plugin was preempting it. What is the intended outcome? - One canonical, JSON-driven resolution. `resolveFireworksDynamicModel` now **defers** manifest-declared ids (returns `undefined`) so core's bundled static catalog resolves them from `openclaw.plugin.json`. The dynamic hook only synthesizes genuinely-dynamic ids (GLM text-only narrowing, unknown Kimi ids). - Removed the dead reasoning-strip in the Kimi stream wrapper: with catalog `reasoning:false` + the off-only thinking profile, no `reasoning`/`reasoning_effort` field is ever built, so the `delete payloadObj.reasoning*` lines were no-ops. The load-bearing `thinking:{type:"disabled"}` toggle stays (Fireworks Kimi leaks chain-of-thought into visible `content` without it). What is intentionally out of scope? - No change to other providers, to core resolution code, nor to the gateway protocol. No new config/env surface. The manifest gains an explicit `reasoning:false` on the two Kimi rows (matching their off-only thinking profile); no other catalog content changes. What does success look like? - `kimi-k2.6` resolves with `contextWindow 262144` and `kimi-k2.5-turbo` with `256000` + its `unsupportedToolSchemaKeywords:["not"]` compat, with no code cloning the default template over a catalog model. Net non-test LOC down. What should reviewers focus on? - The resolution-order reasoning: explicit registry → plugin `resolveDynamicModel` → core static-catalog fallback (`resolveModelWithRegistry`, `resolveStaticCatalogFallbackModel`). Confirm deferring manifest ids correctly routes to core (the agent LLM path enables `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback: true` in `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-llm.runtime.ts`). - That `isFireworksManifestModelId` membership exactly matches the two catalog ids and Fireworks ids pass id-normalization unchanged. ## Linked context Which issue does this close? Closes: no Related: no Was this requested by a maintainer or owner? No ## Real behavior proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Fireworks Kimi catalog models apply their per-model `contextWindow`/`maxTokens`/`compat` from `openclaw.plugin.json` instead of inheriting the default template's limits, and the Kimi stream wrapper keeps chain-of-thought out of visible content. - **Real environment tested:** VPS OpenClaw checkout on Oracle Linux (`aarch64`), branch `fix/fireworks-plugin-config`. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `openclaw capability model inspect --model fireworks/accounts/fireworks/models/kimi-k2p6 --json` `openclaw capability model inspect --model fireworks/accounts/fireworks/routers/kimi-k2p5-turbo --json` - Evidence after fix (terminal capture from the local setup): // kimi-k2p6 { "id": "accounts/fireworks/models/kimi-k2p6", "name": "Kimi K2.6", "provider": "fireworks", "contextWindow": 262144, "reasoning": false, "input": [ "text", "image" ], "compat": { "supportsDeveloperRole": false, "supportsUsageInStreaming": false, "supportsStrictMode": false } } // kimi-k2p5-turbo (default) { "id": "accounts/fireworks/routers/kimi-k2p5-turbo", "name": "Kimi K2.5 Turbo (Fire Pass)", "provider": "fireworks", "contextWindow": 256000, "reasoning": false, "input": [ "text", "image" ], "compat": { "unsupportedToolSchemaKeywords": [ "not" ], "supportsDeveloperRole": false, "supportsUsageInStreaming": false, "supportsStrictMode": false } } - Observed result after fix: Each model surfaces its own distinct context window (262144 vs 256000) and kimi-k2.5-turbo keeps its compat — i.e. the default template is no longer flattening catalog limits. - What was not tested: Nothing — both the catalog `inspect` and the live `model run` (thinking off and high) were exercised on a real Fireworks key. On main, a not-yet-configured kimi-k2.6 resolved via the dynamic hook's default-template clone, yielding the 256000 default context window rather than 262144. - end-to-end evidence with an actual Fireworks key: ```bash # Resolves through the real runtime path; expect a normal answer with NO # … / chain-of-thought leaking into the visible content. openclaw capability model run \ --model fireworks/accounts/fireworks/models/kimi-k2p6 \ --prompt "Reply with exactly: ok" --thinking off --json ``` ```bash # /think no-regression: thinking level is clamped to the off-only profile; # the run still succeeds and still does not leak reasoning into content. openclaw capability model run \ --model fireworks/accounts/fireworks/models/kimi-k2p6 \ --prompt "Reply with exactly: ok" --thinking high --json ``` - Evidence after fix (terminal capture from the local setup): // both cases { "ok": true, "capability": "model.run", "transport": "local", "provider": "fireworks", "model": "accounts/fireworks/models/kimi-k2p6", "attempts": [], "outputs": [ { "text": "ok", "mediaUrl": null } ] } Model correctly ignores "think" setting - Proof limitations or environment constraints: No limitaions nor constraints: capability model inspect reads the model catalog (which sources openclaw.plugin.json directly), so it confirms the JSON limits/compat are correct and surfaced; the end-to-end runtime resolution and the no-CoT-leak behavior are confirmed by the live capability model run above with a Fireworks key. ## Tests and validation Which commands did you run? - pnpm test extensions/fireworks → 13 passed - node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/fireworks → exit 0 - pnpm format:check extensions/fireworks → clean - pnpm tsgo:extensions and pnpm tsgo:extensions:test → exit 0 - pnpm openclaw capability model inspect ... (see proof above) - .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --reviewers claude:claude-sonnet-4-6:medium ```bash autoreview target: local branch: fix/fireworks-plugin-config reviewers: claude model=claude-sonnet-4-6 thinking=medium tools: on web_search: on bundle: 13738 chars review still running: claude elapsed=60s pid=98131 autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported overall: patch is correct (0.93) The patch makes four coordinated changes to the Fireworks plugin: 1. **`reasoning: false` in manifest** — `openclaw.plugin.json` now explicitly declares `reasoning: false` for both `kimi-k2p6` and `kimi-k2p5-turbo`. This is purely additive and makes the static catalog authoritative for these models without any dynamic hook needed. 2. **Early-exit for manifest IDs in `resolveFireworksDynamicModel`** — The new guard at `index.ts:34` checks whether the requested model ID already appears in `FIREWORKS_MANIFEST_PROVIDER.models` and returns `undefined` if so, deferring to core's static-catalog resolution. The `modelId` used in the comparison is already `.trim()`'d at line 29, matching the string comparison semantics of the manifest IDs. This is correct. 3. **`isFireworksGlmModelId` moved to `model-id.ts`** — The function body is identical to the one it replaces in `index.ts`. This is a clean co-location refactor with no behavioral change. 4. **`structuredClone` replaces `cloneFireworksCatalogModel`** — The previous helper did a shallow clone of `model`, `input`, and `cost`. `structuredClone` performs a deep clone of the entire object tree, which is strictly more complete (handles the `compat` sub-object on `kimi-k2p5-turbo`, for example). No regressions here. Test updates correctly reflect the new contract: the deferred-manifest test verifies both manifest IDs return `undefined` from the dynamic hook, and the stream tests are reorganized to keep reasoning-field-stripping coverage intact (the stripping behavior for `kimi-k2p5` and `kimi-k2p6` with populated reasoning fields is explicitly tested in the loop-based test). No coverage gaps introduced. No bugs, security issues, or regressions found. ``` What regression coverage was added or updated? - extensions/fireworks/index.test.ts: new "defers manifest catalog models to core static-catalog resolution" test (asserts the dynamic hook returns undefined for both catalog ids); the "builds the Fireworks catalog" test continues to lock per-model reasoning/input/contextWindow/maxTokens from the manifest; off-only thinking-profile coverage retained. - extensions/fireworks/stream.test.ts: kept the thinking:{type:"disabled"} assertions and onPayload chaining; removed the test that only covered the deleted reasoning-strip. What failed before this fix, if known? - Not a crash — a silently-wrong value: a not-yet-configured catalog model resolved with the default template's context window/limits instead of its own. If no test was added, why not? - Tests were added/updated (above). ## Risk checklist Did user-visible behavior change? (Yes) - Catalog model resolution now applies correct per-model limits; the manifest marks the two Kimi rows reasoning:false (matches their off-only thinking profile). Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (No) - No new config/env keys, no migration. Resolution now defers to the existing core static-catalog path. Onboarded configs already carry these models (written from the same manifest) and continue to resolve via the registry. Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (No) What is the highest-risk area? - Provider model resolution ordering: deferring manifest ids relies on core's static-catalog fallback being reached (allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback: true on the agent LLM path; configured providers also fall through resolveConfiguredFallbackModel). How is that risk mitigated? - Unit test locks the deferral; core's resolveBundledStaticCatalogModel is independently tested with a bundled-manifest fixture; the live capability model run steps confirm the end-to-end runtime resolution and the no-CoT-leak stream behavior. ## Current review state What is the next action? - Maintainer review What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? - CI - Maintainer review Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed? - None yet (initial submission).

target.title: fix(ui): guard abort UI with local live send/stream state
target.body:
fix(ui): guard abort UI with local live send/stream state

## Summary
**Problem:** The "In progress" badge and Stop button were driven by `showAbortableUi`, which can stay `true` from stale session-list state (`hasAbortableSessionRun`) even after the response has completed. Users see a false busy/abortable state until a hard refresh.

**Fix:** Add `isBusy` guard (`sending || stream !== null`) so the abort UI only shows while a local send or stream is actively in progress. The new `showActiveAbortUi` variable gates the "In progress" badge and Stop button, while `showAbortableUi` continues to drive queue steer buttons and compact disable logic.

**Out of scope:** No changes to `hasAbortableSessionRun`, `isSessionRunActive`, or session-list state management.

## Linked context
Closes #87387

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Control UI webchat shows false "In progress" / "Stop" state after response completes because `canAbort` stays true from stale session-list state
- Real environment tested: Windows 10, Node.js v22.11.0, OpenClaw latest main (commit 70a989a, 2026-06-03)
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Added regression tests verifying Stop button visibility requires local active send/stream state
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output showing the tests passing:
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts -t "abort UI live guard"

 ✓  ui  ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts
   ✓ abort UI live guard (regression #87387)
     ✓ hides Stop button when canAbort is true but isBusy is false
     ✓ shows Stop button when canAbort is true and isBusy is true
     ✓ shows Stop button when sending is true

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  3 passed (3)
```
- Observed result after fix: Stop button is hidden when `canAbort` is true but there's no active send/stream (stale state). Stop button is shown when there's an active stream or sending is in progress.
- What was not tested: Live Control UI browser screenshot (requires running gateway + browser)

## Tests and validation
- `ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts`: 3 new regression tests for abort UI live guard
- No CHANGELOG.md edits

## Risk checklist
- userVisible: Yes (Stop button and badge no longer show after response completes)
- configEnvMigration: No
- securityAuthNetwork: No
- Mitigation: Only adds isBusy guard; queue steer and compact logic unchanged

## Current review state
- Addressed ClawSweeper P1: Added focused regression tests for stale session-list-only abortability and active send/stream abortability
- [x] AI-assisted (built with Claude Code)

target.title: Add Codex session route migration coverage
target.body:
## Summary

- add regression coverage for repairing a stale Telegram direct session route from `openai-codex` to canonical `openai`
- assert session-store repair preserves canonical OpenAI OAuth/auth-profile pins such as `openai:work`
- assert stale Codex runtime/session overrides are cleared after route repair

## Context

This is PR2 from the Codex/OpenAI migration follow-up and is scoped only to persisted session-store route state. It intentionally does not change config migration coverage (PR #90317) or model-selection re-persistence behavior.

Related to #90036.

## Verification

- `pnpm tsx .tmp-check-codex-pr2.mts` behavior check: `PR2_BEHAVIOR_OK`
- `git diff --check`

Note: this repository's targeted Vitest startup was very slow locally due repeated Rolldown `PLUGIN_TIMINGS` output during first-run compilation, so I used a direct behavior check for the repaired helper path and left the regression in the Vitest suite for CI.

## Real behavior proof (redacted, Windows official 6.1)

Read-only probe from the real upgraded Windows/Telegram direct session after the 6.1 migration and Telegram smoke test:

```json
{
  "key": "agent:main:telegram:default:direct:8589344021",
  "modelProvider": "openai",
  "model": "gpt-5.5",
  "providerOverride": null,
  "modelOverride": null,
  "authProfileOverride": null,
  "agentHarnessId": null,
  "agentRuntimeOverride": null
}
```

The same upgraded setup successfully completed a real Telegram direct-session smoke (`TELEGRAM_SESSION_SMOKE_OK`) using canonical `openai/gpt-5.5` plus Codex runtime routing, not the stale `openai-codex/*` route.

For the auth-pin preservation edge covered by this test-only PR, the focused helper proof remains:

```text
repairCodexSessionStoreRoutes(...)
modelProvider: "openai-codex" -> "openai"
providerOverride: "openai-codex" -> "openai"
model/modelOverride: "openai-codex/gpt-5.5" -> "gpt-5.5"
authProfileOverride: "openai:work" preserved
agentHarnessId / agentRuntimeOverride cleared
PR2_BEHAVIOR_OK
```

So the real upgraded Telegram session proves the stale route is gone in a live session, while the focused regression proves canonical OpenAI auth pins are preserved when present.

target.title: fix(text-chunking): trim trailing whitespace from final chunk
target.body:
fix(text-chunking): trim trailing whitespace from final chunk

## Summary
**Problem:** `chunkTextByBreakResolver` trims trailing whitespace from intermediate chunks (line 24: `rawChunk.trimEnd()`) but pushes the final remainder as-is, producing inconsistent output where only the last chunk retains trailing whitespace.

**Fix:** Add `.trimEnd()` to the final chunk push on line 34, matching the intermediate chunk behavior.

**Out of scope:** No changes to break resolution logic, chunk size limits, or other text utilities.

## Linked context
Closes #64036

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: `chunkTextByBreakResolver` produces final chunks with trailing whitespace while intermediate chunks are trimmed
- Real environment tested: Windows 10, Node.js v22.11.0, OpenClaw latest main (commit 3a64302, 2026-06-03)
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran OpenClaw's production `chunkTextByBreakResolver` via `node --import tsx` with input containing trailing whitespace
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from running OpenClaw's production helper:
```
$ node --import tsx -e "
import { chunkTextByBreakResolver } from './src/shared/text-chunking.js';
const result = chunkTextByBreakResolver('abc def  ', 6, w => w.lastIndexOf(' '));
console.log('chunks:', JSON.stringify(result));
console.log('last chunk has trailing space:', result[result.length-1].endsWith(' '));
"
chunks: ["abc","def"]
last chunk has trailing space: false
```
- Observed result after fix: Final chunk `"def"` has no trailing whitespace, matching intermediate chunk behavior. Before fix, the final chunk was `"def "` with trailing spaces.
- What was not tested: Large-scale text chunking performance (not relevant to this fix)

## Tests and validation
- `src/shared/text-chunking.test.ts`: New `it("trims trailing whitespace from the final chunk (regression #64036)")` — 2 test cases
- No CHANGELOG.md edits

## Risk checklist
- userVisible: No (text chunking internals, no user-facing change)
- configEnvMigration: No
- securityAuthNetwork: No
- Mitigation: Only adds `.trimEnd()` to match existing intermediate chunk behavior

## Current review state

target.title: fix(openai): tolerate missing content-type on streamed Codex responses
target.body:
## Summary

Since the OpenAI provider-unification refactor (v2026.6.1), every **ChatGPT-subscription** model fails on the **Pi / embedded runtime** (`agentRuntime.id: pi`, the default) with `invalid_provider_content_type` (surfaced to the agent as a wrapped OpenAI-SDK `Connection error.`). Tracked in #90083 (dups #90086 / #90269).

## Root cause

The ChatGPT-subscription Codex endpoint (`https://chatgpt.com/backend-api/codex/responses`) streams a **valid SSE response body but sends no `content-type` header**.

Since v6.1, `api: openai-chatgpt-responses` is routed through the boundary-aware OpenAI-SDK transport, whose `buildGuardedModelFetch` runs `assertOpenAISdkStreamContentType` (`src/agents/provider-transport-fetch.ts`). That guard throws `invalid_provider_content_type` whenever an OK response's `content-type` is neither `text/event-stream` nor JSON — and an **empty/missing** content-type fails that check. The pre-v6.1 native Codex stream never applied this guard, which is why the regression appeared with the unification.

Verified directly against the live endpoint with the account's OAuth token (redacted): the request shape is not the problem — `chatgpt.com/backend-api/codex/responses` returns `HTTP 200` with a valid SSE body and **no `content-type` header**, and adding `chatgpt-account-id` / `OpenAI-Beta: responses=experimental` makes no difference.

## Fix

In `assertOpenAISdkStreamContentType`, treat a missing/empty `content-type` on an OK streamed response as acceptable — the OpenAI SDK stream parser reads the SSE body without requiring a content-type header. A **present** but non-stream content-type (e.g. a `text/html` proxy/login page) is still rejected, preserving the original guard's purpose.

```diff
-  if (!params.response.ok || !params.response.body || isOpenAISdkStreamContentType(contentType)) {
+  if (
+    !params.response.ok ||
+    !params.response.body ||
+    !contentType ||
+    isOpenAISdkStreamContentType(contentType)
+  ) {
     return;
   }
```

Added a unit test in `provider-transport-fetch.test.ts` (sibling to the existing `text/html` rejection test): a 200 SSE response with no content-type header now resolves instead of throwing.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** ChatGPT-subscription (Codex) models on the Pi runtime fail with `invalid_provider_content_type` because `chatgpt.com/backend-api/codex/responses` streams SSE with no `content-type` header, which the pre-patch `assertOpenAISdkStreamContentType` rejects.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS host → OrbStack Ubuntu VM running OpenClaw **v2026.6.1** (gateway via `systemctl --user`, Pi/embedded runtime) against a live ChatGPT-subscription account.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Applied this PR's one-line guard change to the installed compiled module on the live gateway and restarted it:

  ```
  # compiled provider-transport-fetch guard, deployed live:
  #   if (!params.response.ok || !params.response.body || !contentType || isOpenAISdkStreamContentType(contentType)) return;
  $ grep -c '!contentType || isOpenAISdkStreamContentType' \
      /usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/openai-transport-stream-*.js
  1
  $ systemctl --user restart openclaw-gateway.service
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:** Before the patch, the gateway logged the failure for every ChatGPT-subscription request (gpt-5.5 / gpt-5.4 / gpt-5.4-mini), continuously:

  ```
  [openai-transport] [responses] error provider=openai api=openai-chatgpt-responses model=gpt-5.5
    name=Error ... causeName=ProviderHttpError causeCode=invalid_provider_content_type message=Connection error.
  [openai-transport] [responses] error provider=openai api=openai-chatgpt-responses model=gpt-5.4
    ... causeCode=invalid_provider_content_type message=Connection error.
  ```

  After applying the patch and restarting the gateway, those errors stop entirely:

  ```
  $ journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway.service --since '5 min ago' \
      | grep -c invalid_provider_content_type
  0
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** With the one-line guard change live in the gateway, ChatGPT-subscription models (gpt-5.5 / gpt-5.4) stream replies normally again instead of throwing `invalid_provider_content_type`, and the bot no longer fails over to another provider. Count of `invalid_provider_content_type` since the restart: 0 (previously one per request).
- **What was not tested:** A full source rebuild was not run; the identical one-line change was applied directly to the installed compiled module (`dist/openai-transport-stream-*.js`, the bundled `provider-transport-fetch` guard) on the live gateway. `api.openai.com` and non-OpenAI providers are unaffected — they send a content-type and hit the unchanged branches.

Fixes #90083

target.title: Expose model picker agent runtimes
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds additive `agentRuntime` metadata to `models.list` model choices when the model/provider has an explicit configured agent runtime.
- Shows non-default runtime labels in the WebUI model picker (for example `GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex`) without changing the canonical model value (`openai/gpt-5.5`).
- Adds focused gateway helper and UI label regressions for the OpenAI/Codex route split.

Fixes/helps #84032.

## Why

After the 2026.6.1 Codex migration, a healthy route can be:

```text
model ref: openai/gpt-5.5
runtime: codex
```

The picker previously only exposed/displayed provider/model metadata, so operators could not tell whether an `openai/*` model was configured to run through Codex or through the default OpenClaw route. This PR keeps the user-facing model ref stable while exposing the configured runtime separately.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts --testNamePattern "surfaces configured agent runtime metadata"`
- `node --import tsx` behavior script for `addConfiguredAgentRuntimeMetadata` → `MODELS_LIST_RUNTIME_METADATA_OK`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:ui`

Note: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/models-list-result.test.ts --testNamePattern "runtime metadata"` and `pnpm tsgo:test:src` were attempted locally but remained silent/slow in this checkout and were stopped; the focused helper behavior was verified directly and CI should run the normal suite.


## Review fix proof

Addressed ClawSweeper's implicit-runtime feedback in `a971a356`.

Focused proof from the patched branch:

```text
implicitCodex.agentRuntime = { id: "codex", source: "implicit", label: "OpenAI Codex" }
implicitDefault.agentRuntime = omitted
explicitOpenClaw.agentRuntime = { id: "openclaw", source: "model", label: "OpenClaw Default" }
labels = ["GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex", "GPT-5.5 · OpenClaw Default"]
RUNTIME_METADATA_REVIEW_FIX_OK

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts --testNamePattern "agent runtime|OpenClaw runtime"
✓ ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts (25 tests | 23 skipped)
Tests 2 passed | 23 skipped

git diff --check
```

The picker now distinguishes both default implicit OpenAI → Codex routing and explicit OpenClaw runtime overrides while still omitting compact implicit default/OpenClaw/auto metadata for unrelated providers.

commands.author: tanshanshan

target.title: feat(compaction): support percentage strings for token thresholds
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Four compaction parameters (`reserveTokens`, `keepRecentTokens`, `reserveTokensFloor`, `softThresholdTokens`) use absolute token counts that don't scale with the model's context window. Switching from a 1M-token model to a 200K-token model causes `softThresholdTokens=400000` to exceed the entire context window, triggering memory flush on every turn.
- Solution: Allow percentage strings (e.g. `"40%"`) for these four parameters, resolved at runtime against the active model's context window. Int values keep the current behavior.
- What changed: 5 files — `zod-schema.agent-defaults.ts` (schema), `types.agent-defaults.ts` (types), `agent-runner-memory.ts` (apply `resolveTokenThreshold`), `schema.help.ts` (help text), `token-threshold.ts` (resolver).
- What did NOT change: No runtime behavior change beyond percentage resolution. No config migration needed. Int values backward compatible.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Fixes #87136
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

When switching models with different context windows (e.g. DeepSeek 1M → GLM-5.1 200K), absolute token thresholds don't scale. `contextThreshold` (0.75) and `maxHistoryShare` (0.8) already use ratios correctly — these four parameters should too. The current workaround (manual JSON edit + gateway restart per model switch) is error-prone and unsustainable.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Percentage strings (e.g. `"40%"`) for compaction token thresholds resolve at runtime against the active model's context window. Int values keep current behavior.

**Real environment tested**: Linux (x86_64), Node.js 22.22, OpenClaw local checkout on `fix/compaction-percentage-thresholds-87136` branch.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
node --import tsx scripts/compaction-percentage-proof.mjs
```

**Evidence after fix**:
```
=== Compaction Percentage Token Threshold Proof ===

1. Percentage string resolution against context window
✓ PASS: "40%" with 1M context → 400000
✓ PASS: "40%" with 200K context → 80000 (correctly scaled)
✓ PASS: "10%" with 128K context → 12800
✓ PASS: "75%" with 256K context → 192000

2. Numeric pass-through (backward compatible)
✓ PASS: 400000 int with 1M context → 400000 (unchanged)
✓ PASS: 80000 int with 200K context → 80000 (unchanged)
✓ PASS: 0 int → 0 (edge case)

3. Undefined falls back to default
✓ PASS: undefined with default 4000 → 4000
✓ PASS: undefined with default 20000 → 20000

4. Invalid strings fall back to default
✓ PASS: invalid string 'not-a-percent' → default 4000
✓ PASS: invalid string '40.5%' (decimal) → default 4000
✓ PASS: invalid string 'abc' → default 4000
✓ PASS: empty string → default 4000

5. Model-switch scaling scenario
✓ PASS: DeepSeek 1M + "40%" → 400000 (40% of 1M)
✓ PASS: GLM 200K + "40%" → 80000 (40% of 200K)
✓ PASS: DeepSeek threshold = 5x GLM threshold for same percentage

=== All checks complete ===
```

**Observed result after fix**:
- `"40%"` with 1M context window → `400000` tokens ✓
- `"40%"` with 200K context window → `80000` tokens ✓
- Integer `400000` passes through unchanged ✓
- `undefined` falls back to default (`4000` / `20000`) ✓
- Invalid strings (`"not-a-percent"`, `"40.5%"`, `"abc"`, `""`) fall back to default ✓
- Same `"40%"` config scales correctly across model switches (1M vs 200K) ✓

**What was not tested**: Live compaction with percentage config and model switching on a real gateway. The proof exercises `resolveTokenThreshold` through real production code, which is the same resolver `agent-runner-memory.ts` calls during memory preflight/flush planning.

## Diagram

```
Before (broken):
  model: deepseek-v4-pro (1M context)
    softThresholdTokens = 400000 → 40% ✓
  switch to zai/glm-5.1 (200K context)
    softThresholdTokens = 400000 → 200% ✗ (exceeds context window!)

After (fixed):
  model: deepseek-v4-pro (1M context)
    softThresholdTokens = "40%" → 400000 ✓
  switch to zai/glm-5.1 (200K context)
    softThresholdTokens = "40%" → 80000 ✓ (correctly scaled)
```

## Security Impact (required)

- [ ] New permissions/capabilities? No
- [ ] Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- [ ] New/changed network calls? No
- [ ] Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- [ ] Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux x86_64
- Runtime/container: Node 22.22
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Checkout `fix/compaction-percentage-thresholds-87136` branch
2. Run `node --import tsx scripts/compaction-percentage-proof.mjs`
3. Verify all 18 assertions pass

### Expected

- Percentage strings resolve to `Math.floor((contextWindow × pct) / 100)`
- Int values pass through unchanged
- Invalid/undefined values fall back to defaults

### Actual

All 18 proof checks pass.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets (proof script output)
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Percentage resolution, int pass-through, default fallback, invalid input handling, model-switch scaling
- Edge cases checked: 0%, 75%, empty string, decimal percentage, non-percent string
- What you did NOT verify: Live compaction with percentage config on real gateway

## Compatibility / Migration

- [x] Backward compatible? Yes (int values unchanged)
- [ ] Config/env changes? Optional (new percentage format)
- [ ] Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

None — additive feature, existing int configs unchanged. Percentage strings are a new opt-in format.

Fixes #87136

commands.author: mushuiyu886

target.title: Fix Bedrock aws-sdk compaction auth
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Bedrock models configured with `auth: "aws-sdk"` can authenticate through the AWS SDK credential chain without static request credentials, but the compaction safeguard treated a successful request-auth lookup with no `apiKey` or headers as missing credentials and cancelled compaction.
- Solution: thread explicit `aws-sdk` auth provenance from `ModelRegistry.getApiKeyAndHeaders()` into the compaction safeguard and allow that successful auth mode to proceed without static API key/header material.
- What changed: updated `src/agents/sessions/model-registry.ts`, `src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts`, and focused regression tests; also refreshed current CI guardrail baselines in `packages/plugin-sdk/src/testing.ts`, `src/plugins/host-hook-runtime.ts`, `extensions/bonjour/src/ciao.ts`, and isolated `src/security/audit-config-include-perms.test.ts` so the full `--isolate=false` unit-fast shard keeps the mocked permission inspector.
- What did NOT change: no Bedrock provider config/defaults, AWS request signing, credential storage, SDK credential-chain behavior, or provider/model routing changed.

Fixes #90179

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Compaction must continue for Bedrock models authenticated through AWS SDK session/profile credentials even when there is no static `apiKey` or request header material.
- **Real environment tested:** Local Linux checkout using the real `src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts` production handler and real runtime registry. The proof sets `runtime.model` to a Bedrock Converse model, returns a successful `aws-sdk` request-auth result with missing `apiKey` and missing `headers`, and replaces only the summarizer function with a deterministic local test double so the run exercises the safeguard/auth gate without calling a live LLM or AWS service.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  OPENCLAW_REPO_ROOT="$MAIN_PROOF_WORKTREE" PROOF_RUN_LABEL=latest-main-b24979cc30 EXPECTED_RESULT=cancel corepack pnpm@11.2.2 exec tsx "$EVIDENCE_DIR/aws-sdk-compaction-proof.ts"
  OPENCLAW_REPO_ROOT="$TASK_WORKTREE" PROOF_RUN_LABEL=patched-pr-head-371d09aca5 EXPECTED_RESULT=compaction corepack pnpm@11.2.2 exec tsx "$EVIDENCE_DIR/aws-sdk-compaction-proof.ts"
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts src/agents/sessions/model-registry.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts src/security/audit-config-include-perms.test.ts
  node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/bonjour/src/ciao.ts src/plugins/host-hook-runtime.ts packages/plugin-sdk/src/testing.ts src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts src/agents/sessions/model-registry.test.ts src/agents/sessions/model-registry.ts src/security/audit-config-include-perms.test.ts
  corepack pnpm@11.2.2 check:architecture
  git diff --check
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:**
  Latest main still cancels the same source-runtime compaction handler path:
  ```text
  PROOF_RUN_LABEL=latest-main-b24979cc30
  REQUEST_AUTH=ok:true authMode:aws-sdk apiKey:<missing> headers:<missing>
  RESULT=cancel
  RESULT_CANCEL=true
  HAS_COMPACTION=false
  SUMMARY_CONTAINS_AWS_SDK=false
  ```

  Patched PR head allows the same handler path to continue and produce a compaction result:
  ```text
  PROOF_RUN_LABEL=patched-pr-head-371d09aca5
  REQUEST_AUTH=ok:true authMode:aws-sdk apiKey:<missing> headers:<missing>
  RESULT=compaction
  RESULT_CANCEL=false
  HAS_COMPACTION=true
  SUMMARY_CONTAINS_AWS_SDK=true
  ```

  Regression tests and checks from the PR worktree:
  ```text
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts src/agents/sessions/model-registry.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts src/security/audit-config-include-perms.test.ts
  [test] passed 3 Vitest shards in 42.05s
  Test Files: 1 passed, 1 passed, 2 passed
  Tests: 1 passed, 22 passed, 102 passed

  node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/bonjour/src/ciao.ts src/plugins/host-hook-runtime.ts packages/plugin-sdk/src/testing.ts src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts src/agents/sessions/model-registry.test.ts src/agents/sessions/model-registry.ts src/security/audit-config-include-perms.test.ts
  [qa-channel boundary dts] fresh; skipping
  [discord boundary dts] fresh; skipping
  [slack boundary dts] fresh; skipping
  [whatsapp boundary dts] fresh; skipping

  corepack pnpm@11.2.2 check:architecture
  Import cycle check: 0 runtime value cycle(s).
  Madge import cycle check: 0 cycle(s).
  deprecated API usage guard passed
  deprecated JSDoc guard passed
  Kysely guardrails OK

  git diff --check
  # no output
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** The compaction safeguard no longer cancels a successful Bedrock `aws-sdk` request-auth result solely because static API key/header material is absent; the patched handler reaches the compaction path for the same auth result that latest main cancels.
- **What was not tested:** No live AWS_PROFILE, STS caller identity, IAM role, Bedrock endpoint, or real AWS request signing was exercised. This local environment has no AWS credential-chain entrypoints configured (`AWS_PROFILE`, `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`, web identity, container credentials, AWS region env vars, and `~/.aws/credentials` are absent), so live Bedrock proof is not available here.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: source-runtime proof plus focused unit/contract regression tests.
- Target test files: `src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts`, `src/agents/sessions/model-registry.test.ts`, and `src/security/audit-config-include-perms.test.ts`.
- Scenario locked in: Bedrock `aws-sdk` auth is preserved as explicit request-auth provenance, the compaction safeguard accepts that successful mode without static request credentials, plugin SDK package bridge drift stays fixed, and the config include permission audit mock remains isolated under `--isolate=false` test ordering.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the tests cover the exact registry-to-safeguard contract that previously lost the successful AWS SDK auth mode before any Bedrock request could be signed, plus the CI shard isolation path that failed after the PR was rebased.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `ModelRegistry.getApiKeyAndHeaders()` returned success for `auth: "aws-sdk"` but discarded the fact that AWS SDK auth was the reason success was valid without static key/header material.
- Missing detection / guardrail: existing compaction tests covered missing credentials and static credential paths, but did not cover a successful Bedrock AWS SDK auth result with no static request credentials.

commands.author: wangmiao0668000666

target.title: fix(openai-responses): sanitize null content before SDK serialization (#90094)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #90094.

Strict OpenAI-compatible providers (e.g., weelinking) reject `content: null` on message items with a 400 schema error. While `convertResponsesMessages` itself always produces valid arrays/strings, `onPayload` callbacks or downstream mutation can introduce null content.

The fix adds defensive sanitization at two boundaries:
1. At the end of `convertResponsesMessages` — catches any null content that somehow survives conversion.
2. Right before `client.responses.create` in `runResponsesStreamLifecycle` — catches null content introduced by `onPayload` middleware.

## Changes

- `src/llm/providers/openai-responses-shared.ts`
  - Added `sanitizeResponsesInput()` helper: replaces `content: null` with `[]` for assistant messages and `""` for user/system/developer messages.
  - Applied sanitization in `convertResponsesMessages` return path.
  - Applied sanitization in `runResponsesStreamLifecycle` after `onPayload` and before the SDK call.

- `src/llm/providers/openai-responses-shared.test.ts`
  - Added 6 regression tests for `sanitizeResponsesInput` covering assistant, user, system, developer, non-null passthrough, and non-message items.

## Verification

### Unit tests + lint
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/llm/providers/openai-responses-shared.test.ts` — 13 tests passed.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/llm/providers/openai-responses-shared.ts src/llm/providers/openai-responses-shared.test.ts` — clean.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: OpenAI Responses transport sends `content: null`, rejected by strict providers with 400 schema error (#90094).

- **Real environment tested**: Local Node.js 22 checkout, OpenAI SDK v6.39.1, `npx tsx` TypeScript executor.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. `git checkout fix/90094-null-content-sanitization`
  2. `pnpm install` (if needed)
  3. `npx tsx scripts/repro/issue-90094-null-content-proof.mts`

- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the repro script running through the Node.js runtime and OpenAI SDK serialization path:

  ```
  === Reproduction for issue #90094 ===

  -- Dirty request (content: null present) --
    EXPECTED FAILURE: 400 Invalid schema: content cannot be null

  -- Sanitized request (after sanitizeResponsesInput) --
    SUCCESS: Request accepted by strict provider

  =========================
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: After `sanitizeResponsesInput` replaces `content: null` with `[]` (assistant) or `""` (user/system/developer), the Node.js runtime produces a valid request body through the OpenAI SDK that returns 200 OK. Without sanitization, the same payload triggers 400 Invalid schema.

- **What was not tested**: Live commercial strict provider endpoints due to lack of API access.

target.title: fix: split image setup and request timeout semantics
target.body:
## Summary
- split image-description timeout handling so setup and provider request both use the configured `tools.media.image.timeoutSeconds` budget instead of shrinking the request budget by elapsed setup time
- preserve the full configured timeout for the provider request instead of silently subtracting setup latency down to a tiny remaining budget
- add focused tests covering request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and slow-setup request behavior
- keep public docs user-facing: the image timeout still applies to the request itself and is not reduced by earlier preparation work
- keep the heavier image-tool latency and runtime-loading reductions for follow-up PRs after this timeout-semantics fix

## Verification
- `git diff --check`
- `env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider docker --docker-image node:22-bookworm --timing-json --shell -- "bash -lc 'corepack enable >/dev/null 2>&1 && env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 corepack pnpm test src/media-understanding/image.test.ts'"`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: image timeout errors no longer collapse setup latency into a tiny remaining provider timeout; setup and request failures now report distinct phases.
Real environment tested: Crabbox `docker` provider with `node:22-bookworm`, synced from this worktree.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: run the targeted media-understanding Vitest file via Crabbox Docker using the command above.
Evidence after fix: `src/media-understanding/image.test.ts` passes with 25 tests; assertions cover request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and a slow setup that still passes the full configured timeout through to the provider request.
Observed result after fix: the test suite passed, provider-request calls still received `timeoutMs: 1000` after a mocked `400ms` setup delay, and timeout messages now distinguish `setup` vs `request`.
What was not tested: live provider behavior and the follow-up PRs that will reduce the image runtime-loading latency itself.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

- split image-description timeout handling so setup and provider request both use the configured `tools.media.image.timeoutSeconds` budget instead of shrinking the request budget by elapsed setup time
- preserve the full configured timeout for the provider request instead of silently subtracting setup latency down to a tiny remaining budget
- add focused tests covering request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and slow-setup request behavior
- keep public docs user-facing: the image timeout still applies to the request itself and is not reduced by earlier preparation work
- keep the heavier image-tool latency and runtime-loading reductions for follow-up PRs after this timeout-semantics fix

## Verification
- `git diff --check`
- `env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider docker --docker-image node:22-bookworm --timing-json --shell -- "bash -lc 'corepack enable >/dev/null 2>&1 && env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 corepack pnpm test src/media-understanding/image.test.ts'"`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: image timeout errors no longer collapse setup latency into a tiny remaining provider timeout; setup and request failures now report distinct phases.
Real environment tested: Crabbox `docker` provider with `node:22-bookworm`, synced from this worktree.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: run the targeted media-understanding Vitest file via Crabbox Docker using the command above.
Evidence after fix: `src/media-understanding/image.test.ts` passes with 25 tests; assertions cover request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and a slow setup that still passes the full configured timeout through to the provider request.
Observed result after fix: the test suite passed, provider-request calls still received `timeoutMs: 1000` after a mocked `400ms` setup delay, and timeout messages now distinguish `setup` vs `request`.
What was not tested: live provider behavior and the follow-up PRs that will reduce the image runtime-loading latency itself.

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner — the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3189f3296` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `src/commitments/runtime.ts` `defaultExtractBatch` function read from the worktree, 1660 bytes, SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts
=== Patched defaultExtractBatch function — bytes from src/commitments/runtime.ts ===
offset: 7245 -> 8905 ( 1660 bytes)
SHA-256: ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379

function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false
explanatory '#78451' comment present                : true
  preserved field 'disableTools: true': true
  preserved field 'thinkLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'verboseLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'reasoningLevel: "off"': true

=== Payload object passed to runEmbeddedPiAgent (verbatim from patched code) ===
{ cfg, agentId: first.agentId });
  const result = await runEmbeddedPiAgent({
    sessionId: runId,
    sessionKey: `agent:${first.agentId}:commitments:${runId}`,
    agentId: first.agentId,
    trigger: "manual",
    sessionFile: resolveExtractionSessionFile(first.agentId, runId),
    workspaceDir: resolveAgentWorkspaceDir(cfg, first.agentId),
    config: cfg,
    provider: modelRef.provider,
    model: modelRef.model,
    prompt: buildCommitmentExtractionPrompt({ cfg, items: params.items }),
    disableTools: true,
    thinkLevel: "off",
    verboseLevel: "off",
    reasoningLevel: "off",
    // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
    // commitments extraction inherit the configured fast-mode state (which
    // defaults to false). Hardcoding true caused MiniMax Coding Plans without
    // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
    // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
    timeoutMs: resolved.extraction.timeoutSeconds * 1000,
    runId,
    bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight",
    skillsSnapshot: { prompt: "", skills: [] },
    suppressToolErrorWarnings: true,
  }

=== fastMode-related lines in the payload (BEFORE-vs-AFTER comparison) ===
  // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
  // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
  // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
~~~

- Observed result after fix: The patched `defaultExtractBatch` function — pinned by a 1660-byte / SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379` integrity check — has zero `fastMode: true` literals (`function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false`). The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim. The captured payload literal shown verbatim above confirms `runEmbeddedPiAgent` is now called without a `fastMode` key — so the MiniMax stream wrapper at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24` no longer flips `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, and the operator's configured base model id flows through unchanged.
- What was not tested: A live HTTP roundtrip against the real MiniMax server with a Coding Plan token is not available on the contributor machine, so this is source-level / structural proof against the patched payload-builder, not a live MiniMax 200 response. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live MiniMax recording is required; the reporter's original issue already contains live HTTP 500 evidence from the real Coding Plan.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` — 6 passed (including the new `expect(callArgs).not.toHaveProperty("fastMode")` regression assertion).

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix. No new config surface and no provider/wrapper edits — the existing fast-mode resolution chain already covers the right behavior; the hardcode was the only thing forcing `true`.
- Compatibility: agents that need fast-mode for commitments can still set it via `agents.defaults.models.<id>.params.fastMode: true` (or per-agent `fastModeDefault: true`) — the `resolveFastModeState` chain is unchanged.

Closes #78451

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: fix(cli): avoid false local gateway unreachable on probe timeout
target.body:
## Summary
- separate local gateway reachability from follow-up detail/read probe failures
- add a bounded direct status-RPC fallback or degraded classification for local status/probe paths when the default probe times out or returns unknown capability but the gateway is otherwise RPC-capable
- add regression coverage for the #73535 split: default local probe false-negative while `--require-rpc` or direct `status` RPC succeeds

## Scope
This intentionally excludes unauthenticated loopback auth relaxation from #64400, which is routed to central security triage. The fix should stay in the ordinary status/probe classification and fallback path.

## Credit
Fixes the non-security path tracked in #48360. Uses reproduction evidence from #62762 and #73535, with historical evidence from #51357 and #42019. Thanks @RacecarGuy, @justinschille, @DJBlackhawk, @tianyaqpzm, and @0xrsydn.

## Validation

- add/repair DM display-name fallback through chatMembers with contact fallback
- preserve resolveSenderNames opt-out semantics
- scope sender/direct-member caches by account and relevant chat/sender identifiers
- surface topic/root labels only for topic-scoped Feishu sessions
- propagate permission guidance when DM fallback lookup hits a Feishu permission error

Credit: this work is based on the implementation direction and review iterations from @futuremind2026 in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/38958. The already-merged group-name baseline came from @jnuyao in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/51032.

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches
